However, the role retains meaningful barriers through physical prototyping, hands-on user testing, cross-functional collaboration with engineers and manufacturers, and the embodied aesthetic judgment required to balance form, ergonomics, and manufacturability in ways AI cannot yet fully replicate.

2172.rationale: Graphic design is almost entirely computer-based work involving image creation, layout, and typography — all areas where AI tools (Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, DALL-E, automated layout systems) are rapidly automating core tasks. While creative direction, client interpretation, and brand strategy retain human value, the volume of routine design work (social media assets, ad variations, basic logos) is being dramatically reduced by AI, meaning significantly fewer designers will be needed to produce the same output.

2173.rationale: Game and digital media designers work entirely digitally using software tools, making much of their workflow susceptible to AI-driven automation — generative AI can already produce concept art, textures, 3D assets, and UI elements at scale, reducing the volume of manual creative work needed. However, the role retains meaningful human value in creative direction, artistic judgment, iteration with teams, and crafting cohesive player experiences, preventing full automation and keeping it below the highest exposure tier.

2179.rationale: Fashion and product designers face significant AI exposure in the conceptual and visualization phases — generative AI tools (Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, parametric design AI) can rapidly produce design variations, mood boards, and digital prototypes, reducing the labor needed for ideation. However, the hands-on physical work involving material testing, prototype fabrication, supplier visits, and tactile craft judgment creates a meaningful barrier that keeps exposure from reaching the highest levels.

2181.rationale: This occupation has a significant physical component requiring on-site visits, specialized measurement equipment (noise meters, gas detectors, particle counters, vibration meters), and direct workplace inspections that AI cannot replace. However, the substantial desk-based portion — data analysis, report writing, risk assessments, and compliance documentation — is highly exposed to AI augmentation, which could reduce the number of professionals needed even if field work remains human-dependent.

2182.rationale: This occupation has a significant physical fieldwork component — on-site inspections, sample collection, and facility assessments — that creates a natural barrier to direct AI automation, similar to registered nurses or field engineers. However, the substantial administrative portion (writing inspection reports, formal decisions, permit handling, documentation) is highly exposed to AI productivity tools, meaning AI could meaningfully reduce the time needed for back-office work and potentially reduce headcount over time.

2183.rationale: Environmental protection professionals combine substantial field work (site inspections, sample collection, emissions measurement) that resists full automation with significant digital tasks (GIS analysis, environmental modeling, report writing, permit review) that are highly AI-amenable. AI can accelerate data analysis, regulatory compliance checks, and report generation, but the physical fieldwork, site-specific judgment, and stakeholder advisory roles provide meaningful barriers to full displacement.

2211.rationale: Specialist physicians have strong natural barriers to AI automation due to the requirement for physical examinations, hands-on procedures (surgery, endoscopies, injections), and direct patient interaction that demand physical presence and tactile skill. However, AI exposure is meaningfully elevated by the substantial digital workload — interpreting imaging, ordering and analyzing lab results, documentation in EHR systems, and clinical decision support — where AI tools are already reshaping productivity and reducing the cognitive burden, making fewer specialists needed for the administrative and diagnostic interpretation components.

2212.rationale: Resident physicians have strong physical barriers to AI automation due to hands-on patient examinations, invasive procedures, and direct clinical care requiring physical presence. However, significant AI exposure exists in the documentation-heavy, diagnostic, and administrative components of their work, where AI tools are rapidly augmenting or automating tasks like journal documentation, image interpretation, and differential diagnosis support.

2213.rationale: AT-läkare work is heavily grounded in physical presence — hands-on patient examinations, procedures like suturing and venipuncture, and real-time clinical decision-making in diverse hospital settings — which creates strong barriers to direct automation. However, significant portions of their work (EHR documentation, reviewing lab results and imaging, writing referrals, and diagnostic reasoning support) are increasingly augmented or partially automatable by AI, placing them in a moderate exposure range similar to other physicians.

2219.rationale: These physicians work in non-clinical roles (occupational health, insurance medicine, pharmaceutical industry, regulatory work) that are heavily documentation-based, involving written assessments, certificates, and review of medical records — tasks where AI can significantly augment or partially automate output. However, medical judgment, legal accountability, and some physical examinations create barriers to full automation, placing this occupation higher than standard physicians but below purely administrative roles.

2221.rationale: Swedish registered nurses perform predominantly hands-on clinical work — administering medications, wound care, catheterization, and blood sampling — that requires physical presence and cannot be automated away, placing a natural ceiling on AI exposure. However, a meaningful portion of their workflow involves digital documentation, clinical decision support, and medication management systems where AI can meaningfully augment or reduce workload, warranting a score slightly above the midpoint for this occupational category.

2222.rationale: Midwives perform highly physical, hands-on clinical work including deliveries, gynecological examinations, and fetal monitoring that fundamentally requires physical presence and manual skill, creating strong barriers to direct AI automation. AI can assist with documentation, decision support, and administrative tasks but cannot replace the core obstetric and reproductive health care duties, placing this occupation in a similar range to other hands-on clinical roles like nurses and care assistants.

2223.rationale: Anaesthesia nurses perform intensely physical, hands-on work — airway management, IV placement, intubation, and real-time patient monitoring during surgery — that fundamentally requires physical presence and manual dexterity that AI cannot replicate. While AI may assist with documentation, drug dosage calculations, and pattern recognition in monitoring data, the core procedural and crisis-response tasks create a strong natural barrier to automation or significant workforce reduction.

2224.rationale: District nurses perform predominantly hands-on, physical work including wound care, injections, catheter care, and in-person clinical assessments during home visits, creating strong barriers to direct AI automation. However, AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, care coordination, digital health monitoring, and clinical decision support, moderately increasing productivity without replacing the physical caregiving role.

2225.rationale: Psychiatric nurses require substantial physical presence for medication administration, crisis intervention, vital signs monitoring, and therapeutic alliance-building — core functions that AI cannot replicate. However, the administrative and documentation components (EHR charting, care plan coordination, structured clinical interviews) face moderate AI exposure, pulling the score slightly above purely hands-on clinical roles.

2226.rationale: Ambulance nurses work in highly unpredictable, physically demanding environments requiring hands-on patient care, real-time critical decision-making, and manual clinical skills like intubation and CPR that cannot be automated. While AI may assist with diagnostic support, EKG interpretation, and documentation, the core work demands physical presence, dexterity, and human judgment in chaotic pre-hospital settings, creating strong barriers to both direct automation and significant productivity-driven workforce reduction.

2227.rationale: Geriatric nurses perform highly physical, patient-facing work including medication administration, wound care, and hands-on assessment of elderly patients with complex conditions, creating strong barriers to direct AI automation. However, the significant documentation and clinical decision-support components (EHR systems, care coordination, medication management) are increasingly AI-assisted, and AI could reduce administrative burden and improve diagnostic accuracy, though the core caregiving role remains deeply human and physically present.

2228.rationale: Intensive care nursing is overwhelmingly physical and hands-on — managing ventilators, catheters, infusion pumps, and responding to acute deterioration at the bedside — creating strong barriers to direct automation. While AI can assist with monitoring, early warning systems, and documentation in EHR systems like Metavision, the critical procedural, tactile, and rapid-response human judgment required at the ICU bedside limits AI's ability to either replace or dramatically reduce the need for these highly specialized nurses.

2231.rationale: Operating room nurses perform highly physical, hands-on work in sterile surgical environments — passing instruments, managing sterile fields, and conducting instrument counts — that requires immediate physical presence and real-time tactile judgment that AI cannot replicate. While AI may assist with documentation and scheduling, the core scrub/instrument nurse role has strong physical and safety-critical barriers to automation, placing it near the lower-middle range of exposure.

2232.rationale: Pediatric nurses perform highly physical, patient-facing work — administering medications, drawing samples, wound care, and monitoring critically ill children — that fundamentally cannot be automated by AI. While AI can assist with documentation, diagnostic support, and administrative tasks, the hands-on clinical care and emotional reassurance required with vulnerable child patients creates a strong barrier to AI displacement.

2233.rationale: School nurses perform largely physical, hands-on tasks — health screenings, vaccinations, and direct student care — that require in-person presence and real-time human interaction with children, which creates strong barriers to automation. While AI can assist with documentation (e.g., auto-populating health records) and triage guidance, the core of the role involves physical examination, building trust with students, and nuanced psychosocial counseling that resists meaningful AI displacement.

2234.rationale: Occupational health nurses perform largely hands-on clinical tasks — audiometry, blood pressure checks, vaccinations, ergonomic workplace assessments — that require physical presence and cannot be automated by AI. However, their documentation, health risk assessments, counseling support, and coordination tasks have moderate AI exposure, raising the score above the baseline for purely physical roles.

2235.rationale: Radiology nurses have significant physical, hands-on components — patient positioning, contrast agent administration, equipment operation, and direct patient care — that create strong barriers to full automation. However, AI is increasingly automating image acquisition optimization, quality checks, and workflow management through RIS/PACS integration, and may reduce staffing needs over time as imaging processes become more automated and efficient.

2239.rationale: Specialist nurses in oncology, orthopedics, urology, and similar fields perform highly physical, patient-facing work including chemotherapy administration, stoma care, and pain management that requires hands-on clinical skills and real-time human presence, creating strong barriers to direct automation. However, AI will meaningfully impact the documentation, treatment planning support, medication management, and coordination tasks embedded in their workflow, modestly increasing productivity and potentially reducing staffing needs at the margins.

2241.rationale: Psychologists face moderate-to-significant AI exposure: administrative tasks like clinical documentation, report writing, and standardized test interpretation are highly automatable, and AI already assists with screening and assessment tools. However, the therapeutic relationship, real-time empathic attunement, and complex clinical judgment in face-to-face or video sessions provide a meaningful barrier to full automation, keeping exposure below the high end of the scale.

2242.rationale: Psychotherapy is deeply relational and depends on human empathy, therapeutic alliance, and nuanced emotional attunement that AI cannot currently replicate, creating a strong barrier to full automation. However, AI can assist with documentation, standardized assessments, treatment planning, and even deliver structured CBT protocols (as already seen in digital therapy apps), plus video-based sessions reduce physical presence requirements — moderately elevating exposure.

2250.rationale: Veterinarians perform highly physical, hands-on work involving animal examination, surgery, and treatment that cannot be automated, creating a strong barrier to direct AI displacement. However, AI can meaningfully assist with diagnostic imaging interpretation, administrative documentation, treatment recommendations, and telemedicine consultations, moderately increasing individual productivity without eliminating the core physical role.

2260.rationale: Dentistry is fundamentally a hands-on, physical occupation requiring manual dexterity with instruments inside a patient's mouth, making direct automation by AI extremely limited in the near term. However, AI does contribute indirectly through diagnostic imaging analysis, treatment planning software, CAD/CAM restoration design, and administrative documentation, providing modest productivity gains without displacing the core clinical role.

2271.rationale: Chiropractic and naprapathic work is fundamentally hands-on, requiring physical manipulation, mobilization, and soft tissue techniques that cannot be performed remotely or automated by AI. While AI may assist with diagnostic imaging analysis, treatment planning support, and administrative documentation, the core value of the profession is irreducibly physical patient contact, limiting AI exposure to marginal productivity gains rather than substantial displacement.

2272.rationale: Physiotherapists perform predominantly hands-on, physical work involving manual therapy, tactile assessment, and direct patient interaction that AI cannot replicate, creating a strong natural barrier to automation. AI may assist with documentation, treatment planning, and movement analysis software, but the core therapeutic work requires physical presence and skilled manual intervention, limiting overall exposure.

2273.rationale: Occupational therapists work primarily in physical, hands-on settings — conducting in-person assessments, prescribing and fitting assistive devices, training patients in their homes or clinics — which creates a strong barrier to direct AI automation. However, AI can assist with documentation, standardized assessment scoring, and treatment planning recommendations, moderately increasing productivity but not replacing the core physical and relational aspects of the role.

2281.rationale: Pharmacists have significant AI exposure in prescription verification, drug interaction checking, and dosage validation — tasks increasingly handled by automated systems — but the physical dispensing, compounding, and direct patient counseling components create meaningful barriers to full automation. The regulatory compliance role, need for professional liability, and patient-facing trust dimension further anchor the occupation at a moderate exposure level, similar to physicians and nurses rather than pure knowledge workers.

2282.rationale: Dieticians combine direct patient consultations, clinical judgment, and interdisciplinary collaboration that require physical presence and interpersonal skills, creating meaningful barriers to full AI automation. However, significant portions of their work — dietary analysis, meal plan generation, nutritional calculations, documentation, and patient education materials — are highly susceptible to AI augmentation, likely reducing the number of practitioners needed as AI tools handle routine assessments and planning tasks.

2283.rationale: Audiologists and speech therapists require significant hands-on patient interaction, physical assessment techniques, and real-time therapeutic exercises that are difficult to automate, providing a strong barrier to full AI displacement. However, AI can assist with hearing aid programming, diagnostic analysis, documentation, and some remote therapy delivery, creating moderate indirect exposure that slightly elevates the score above purely manual occupations.

2284.rationale: Opticians require significant physical presence for hands-on eye examinations, precise facial measurements, lens grinding, and fitting eyewear directly on patients, creating strong barriers to full AI automation. However, AI is increasingly capable of assisting with refraction determination, diagnostic image analysis (OCT), and digital record management, which could reduce the number of opticians needed or shift their role toward more complex cases.

2289.rationale: These health professionals (orthoptists, audiologists, dietitians, medical technology specialists) perform hands-on patient assessments, physical rehabilitation, and specialized treatments requiring direct patient contact, specialized equipment, and clinical judgment in real-world settings — creating strong barriers to direct automation. However, AI can assist with documentation, diagnostic support, nutritional analysis, and some telehealth consultations, creating moderate indirect exposure that slightly elevates the score above purely manual occupations.

2311.rationale: Professors engage heavily in digital knowledge work — literature review, writing, data analysis, grant applications — where AI can significantly augment or partially automate output, and AI-assisted tools are already reshaping research workflows and academic writing. However, core professorial functions like mentoring doctoral students, designing original research programs, navigating institutional politics, and conducting hands-on laboratory work provide meaningful barriers to full AI displacement, keeping exposure moderate-to-high rather than extreme.

2312.rationale: University lecturers face significant AI exposure in research tasks (literature reviews, writing, grading, course preparation) and administrative duties, but physical presence requirements for on-campus teaching, lab supervision, and real-time student mentoring provide a meaningful barrier. The deeply relational and evaluative aspects of academic work — including thesis supervision, nuanced assessment, and intellectual guidance — still require human judgment, though AI will substantially reduce the labor needed for many supporting tasks.

2313.rationale: Research assistants perform a mix of highly AI-exposed tasks (literature reviews, data analysis, statistical modeling, academic writing) and more protected activities (lab work, physical experiments, fieldwork, mentoring students, and conference participation). AI tools like large language models and automated data analysis will significantly accelerate and partially automate the digital components, but the physical, experimental, and interpersonal dimensions of the role provide a meaningful barrier to full automation.

2314.rationale: PhD students perform highly digital work — literature reviews, data analysis, and academic writing — that is substantially reshapeable by AI tools like LLMs, automated analysis pipelines, and citation assistants, pushing exposure high. However, the significant portion who conduct wet-lab experiments, fieldwork, or hands-on physical research, combined with the inherently creative and novel nature of original research and some teaching obligations, provides a meaningful barrier that prevents the score from reaching the highest tier.

2319.rationale: University teachers in miscellaneous fields face significant AI exposure in content creation, grading, research assistance, and administrative tasks, but retain strong barriers in physical classroom presence, hands-on supervision, mentoring relationships, and practical/artistic instruction that requires in-person interaction. The hybrid nature of the role — combining digital lecture preparation and assessment (highly automatable) with face-to-face seminars, studio work, and individualized student guidance (harder to automate) — places them in the mid-range.

2320.rationale: Vocational education teachers are heavily anchored in physical workshop environments where hands-on demonstration of trade skills — welding, patient care techniques, engine repair — cannot be replicated by AI. While AI can assist with lesson planning, assessment documentation, and some theoretical content delivery, the core value of the role lies in embodied expertise and real-time physical instruction, creating a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

2330.rationale: Gymnasielärare work combines substantial computer-based tasks (lesson planning, grading, feedback, administration) that AI can increasingly automate or augment, with mandatory physical classroom presence and real-time student interaction that resist full automation. AI will likely reduce time spent on administrative and assessment tasks and may allow productivity gains, but the core pedagogical and relational role in a physical classroom setting provides a meaningful barrier to displacement.

2341.rationale: Primary school teaching is fundamentally grounded in physical presence, real-time relational interaction with children, and social-emotional development that AI cannot replicate, creating a strong natural barrier to direct automation. However, AI significantly impacts the substantial administrative and preparatory workload — lesson planning, grading, writing individual development plans, and parent communication — making teachers meaningfully more productive and potentially reducing demand at the margins.

2342.rationale: Fritidspedagoger work is fundamentally physical, relational, and presence-dependent — supervising and engaging children aged 6–13 in hands-on activities like crafts, sports, and play in ways that cannot be replicated remotely or automated. The minor digital administrative component (documentation, parent communication) has some AI exposure, but it represents such a small fraction of the role that overall AI displacement potential remains very low.

2343.rationale: Preschool teaching is fundamentally a physical, hands-on occupation requiring constant in-person presence with young children for caregiving, supervision, and play-based learning — tasks AI cannot automate. While AI may assist with documentation, parent communication, and activity planning, the core work of nurturing and educating 1–5 year olds demands human touch, real-time judgment, and emotional attunement that creates a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

2351.rationale: Special teachers work directly with vulnerable students in physical settings, requiring nuanced human judgment, relationship-building, and hands-on support that AI cannot readily replicate — particularly when working with children with disabilities or behavioral challenges. However, significant portions of their work, such as writing action plans, developing adapted materials, conducting documentation, and administrative reporting, are increasingly automatable, creating moderate but not dominant AI exposure.

2352.rationale: The core of this role — individualized guidance conversations and building trust with students navigating major life decisions — requires human empathy, relationship skills, and contextual judgment that AI cannot fully replicate, providing a meaningful barrier to automation. However, significant portions of the work (researching programs, documenting sessions, compiling catalogues, answering routine questions about admissions) are highly automatable, and AI tools could dramatically reduce the number of counsellors needed by handling informational queries and administrative tasks at scale.

2359.rationale: This role involves a mix of digital content creation, curriculum planning, and communication tasks that AI can significantly assist with or automate, but the core delivery of specialized pedagogy—workshops, lectures, museum education, folk education—requires physical presence and human interaction that creates a natural barrier. The balance between digitally-intensive preparation work (high AI exposure) and in-person facilitation and counseling (lower AI exposure) places this occupation in the mid-range.

2411.rationale: Auditing work is highly digitized and data-driven — analyzing financial statements, sampling transactions, and drafting audit reports are tasks where AI can automate large portions of the workflow, especially with tools like IDEA and AI-enhanced audit platforms already emerging. However, the occupation retains meaningful human elements such as professional judgment on materiality and risk, client interviews, regulatory accountability, and the legal signing responsibility (revisorsansvar) that creates a barrier to full automation.

2412.rationale: Financial controllers work almost entirely digitally using Excel, ERP systems, and BI tools to produce budgets, forecasts, and variance analyses — tasks that AI is rapidly automating or augmenting, enabling significant productivity gains with fewer headcount. 2413.rationale: Financial analysts and investment advisers work almost entirely digitally — analyzing data, building models in Excel/Bloomberg, and producing reports — tasks where AI already demonstrates strong capability in pattern recognition, financial modeling, and report generation. While client relationship management and nuanced judgment provide some human barrier, AI tools are rapidly automating the core analytical work and enabling far fewer analysts to cover the same workload, creating strong indirect exposure as well.

2414.rationale: Trading and fund management is almost entirely digital, conducted via computer-based platforms with heavy reliance on quantitative analysis, pattern recognition, and data processing — all areas where AI excels and is already deeply embedded (algorithmic trading, robo-advisors, AI-driven portfolio optimization). While high-stakes judgment, client relationships, and regulatory accountability provide some human value, AI is rapidly automating execution, analysis, and increasingly even strategic allocation decisions, meaning fewer human traders are needed as productivity per person rises dramatically.

2415.rationale: Economists and macro analysts work almost entirely digitally — collecting data, building econometric models in R/Python/Stata, and writing reports — tasks that AI can directly automate or dramatically accelerate, fitting squarely in the high-exposure tier alongside data analysts and editors. The primary barriers to full displacement are the need for nuanced judgment in interpreting geopolitical/institutional context and high-stakes communication with policymakers, but AI will likely reduce the headcount needed for routine forecasting and data processing significantly.

2419.rationale: This role is almost entirely computer-based, involving data compilation, forecasting, and report generation — tasks where AI tools like generative AI, advanced analytics, and automation can directly substitute or dramatically augment the work. While some advisory and contextual judgment components provide modest protection, the highly digitized, data-driven nature of the work places it firmly in the high-exposure range, similar to accountants and financial analysts but slightly higher due to the generalist, report-heavy character of the role.

2421.rationale: This role is heavily computer-based and involves tasks like data analysis, report writing, process mapping, and strategy documentation that AI can increasingly perform or augment significantly, reducing the number of analysts needed. However, the core of the work relies on stakeholder interviews, workshops, change management facilitation, and building organizational trust — human-interaction elements that create a meaningful barrier to full AI substitution.

2422.rationale: Policy administration professionals perform largely desk-based, digital work involving research, data analysis, report writing, and drafting policy recommendations — all tasks where AI tools (LLMs, data analysis assistants, summarization tools) can directly augment or partially automate core outputs. However, significant human judgment, stakeholder navigation, institutional knowledge, and political context are required, providing some buffer against full automation and keeping the score below the highest tier.

2423.rationale: HR specialists work primarily on a computer using digital HR systems, handling tasks like recruitment screening, policy drafting, compliance documentation, and employee communication — all of which AI can substantially automate or augment, reducing headcount needs. However, a meaningful portion of the role involves nuanced human judgment in negotiations with unions, sensitive employee relations, cultural fit assessments, and mediation, which creates a partial barrier to full AI displacement.

2431.rationale: Advertising and marketing professionals work almost entirely digitally — analyzing data, managing ad platforms, creating content, and building reports — all tasks where AI tools (generative AI for content, AI-driven ad optimization, predictive analytics) are rapidly automating or augmenting core functions. While strategic judgment, client relationships, and creative direction provide some buffer, the predominantly computer-based, data-driven nature of the work places it firmly in the high-exposure range.

2432.rationale: PR and communications work is predominantly digital — writing press releases, managing social media, drafting content, and monitoring media — all of which AI can perform or significantly augment, reducing the headcount needed. However, relationship management with journalists, stakeholders, and crisis communication still requires human judgment and trust-building, providing a partial barrier to full automation.

2511.rationale: System analysts and IT architects work almost entirely at a computer producing documentation, diagrams, requirements specifications, and architecture designs — tasks where AI tools (e.g., AI-assisted modeling, automated documentation, LLM-based requirements analysis) are rapidly encroaching. While senior architectural judgment and stakeholder facilitation retain a human element, the high degree of digitally-codified, structured output makes this occupation highly exposed to both direct automation and significant productivity compression that reduces headcount.

2512.rationale: Software and system developers work almost entirely digitally — writing code, debugging, reviewing, and collaborating through computers — placing them squarely in the highest AI-exposure tier. 2513.rationale: Game and digital media developers work entirely on computers using programming, design tools, and engines — all domains where AI is rapidly advancing through code generation, automated asset creation, procedural content generation, and AI-assisted debugging. While creative direction and complex system architecture still benefit from human expertise, AI tools like GitHub Copilot, generative AI for art/audio assets, and automated testing are already significantly reshaping productivity and reducing headcount needs in this field.

2514.rationale: System testers and test managers work entirely at a computer in a digital environment, making them highly exposed to AI automation — AI tools are already capable of generating test cases, executing automated test suites, identifying bugs, and writing test documentation with increasing sophistication. While test managers retain some value in coordinating teams and making strategic testing decisions, the core technical work of designing and running tests is rapidly being augmented or replaced by AI-driven testing platforms, significantly reducing the headcount needed.

2515.rationale: System administrators work almost entirely at a computer managing configurations, permissions, documentation, and system integrations — tasks that AI can increasingly automate or augment, such as automated patch management, AI-driven anomaly detection, and configuration generation. However, the role still requires contextual organizational knowledge, vendor coordination, and judgment about business-specific system behavior, which provides some buffer against full automation.

2516.rationale: IT security specialists work almost entirely digitally—analyzing logs, running vulnerability scans, writing policies, and responding to incidents—making the role highly amenable to AI augmentation and partial automation through AI-driven threat detection, automated incident response, and AI-assisted penetration testing tools. 2519.rationale: This occupation is almost entirely computer-based, involving scripting, system configuration, cloud platforms, and technical problem-solving — all domains where AI tools are rapidly augmenting or replacing human effort. While some physical tasks like hardware handling provide a minor barrier, the core digital and analytical work aligns closely with high-exposure roles like software developers and data analysts.

2611.rationale: Legal work is heavily document-intensive and research-driven, making large portions of a lawyer's workflow — drafting contracts, legal opinions, pleadings, and conducting case law research — highly susceptible to AI automation and productivity enhancement. 2612.rationale: Judges perform substantial screen-based work — legal research, document review, and drafting judgments — that AI can meaningfully augment, potentially reducing the time needed per case or the number of judges required for administrative matters. However, the role carries significant institutional authority, requires courtroom presence, exercises discretionary judgment in adversarial proceedings, and faces strong legal and democratic barriers to automation, anchoring the exposure at a moderate level.

2613.rationale: Prosecutors perform highly document-intensive, analytical work — reviewing evidence, writing indictments, and managing case files in digital systems — that is substantially automatable by AI for drafting, legal research, and evidence analysis. However, significant barriers remain in the form of courtroom advocacy requiring real-time oral argumentation and judgment calls, discretionary charging decisions with ethical and societal weight, and interpersonal interactions with victims, witnesses, and police that require human credibility and trust.

2614.rationale: Business and corporate lawyers perform highly text-intensive, research-driven work — contract drafting, due diligence, compliance analysis — that AI is already demonstrably automating or augmenting, enabling significant productivity gains and reducing headcount needs. However, the irreplaceable elements of client relationship management, high-stakes negotiation, strategic judgment, and accountability in complex M&A or regulatory matters provide meaningful resistance to full displacement, placing them in the high but not top tier.

2615.rationale: This occupation is almost entirely computer-based — legal research, drafting decisions, writing memoranda, and interpreting regulations are all tasks where AI (via legal databases, document drafting, and regulatory analysis tools) can substantially augment or partially automate the workflow, reducing the number of lawyers needed for routine case processing. However, the work involves nuanced judgment in applying public administrative law, advising officials, and navigating institutional and political context, which maintains meaningful human oversight requirements and keeps full automation at bay.

2619.rationale: This role is predominantly digital — drafting contracts, legal research, compliance monitoring, and internal advisory work are all highly amenable to AI augmentation through tools like contract analysis, legal research automation, and regulatory tracking. However, the need for contextual judgment, client trust, internal stakeholder relationships, and accountability in legal advice provides a meaningful human barrier, keeping it just below the highest-exposure tier.

2621.rationale: Museum curators have significant exposure to AI in research, cataloguing, provenance investigation, and writing tasks — areas where AI tools can dramatically accelerate work and reduce staffing needs. However, the physical handling and assessment of artifacts, exhibition design requiring contextual judgment, and community/institutional collaboration provide meaningful barriers to full automation.

2622.rationale: Librarians and archivists have high exposure in their core information organization, cataloging, and research assistance tasks, which AI can increasingly automate through intelligent search, metadata generation, and digital classification systems. However, the occupation retains a meaningful physical component — handling historical documents, managing physical collections, and providing in-person user guidance — which moderates the overall exposure compared to fully digital knowledge work.

2623.rationale: Archaeology involves substantial physical fieldwork — excavations, surveys, and hands-on artifact handling — that AI cannot automate, creating a natural barrier to full AI displacement. However, the significant office and lab-based components (report writing, GIS analysis, artifact classification, literature review, and documentation) are increasingly AI-augmentable, meaning fewer specialists may be needed per project even as field presence remains essential.

2641.rationale: Authors and writers work entirely on computers producing text-based content, which is precisely what modern AI systems excel at generating, editing, and refining. While creative voice and originality provide some human value, AI tools are already dramatically reshaping writing workflows — from ghostwriting and content generation to editing and ideation — meaning both direct automation and productivity-driven workforce reduction are significant threats.

2642.rationale: Journalism is highly exposed to AI as core tasks like drafting articles, summarizing reports, fact-checking, translating, and optimizing content for SEO can be substantially automated or AI-assisted, reducing the number of journalists needed for routine news production. However, investigative work, building source relationships, attending live events, and editorial judgment retain a human dimension that keeps the score below the 8-9 range of pure screen-based roles.

2643.rationale: Translation work is almost entirely digital and remote, and AI (neural machine translation, LLMs) has already dramatically disrupted the field — translators increasingly work as post-editors of AI output rather than producing translations from scratch, meaning far fewer are needed for the same volume. Interpreters retain more protection due to real-time, in-person human interaction requirements, but even here remote interpreting and AI speech translation tools are rapidly encroaching, keeping overall exposure very high.

2651.rationale: Visual artists face significant AI exposure in digital illustration, concept generation, and image creation workflows, where tools like Midjourney and Stable Diffusion can replicate or assist many tasks at scale, reducing demand for commercial illustrators and graphic artists. However, fine artists working with physical media (sculpture, painting, printmaking) retain a meaningful barrier through manual craft, material presence, and the cultural premium placed on human-authored original works, keeping overall exposure moderate rather than high.

2652.rationale: AI tools are significantly reshaping composition, production, and arrangement workflows — generative music AI can now draft backing tracks, harmonies, and full compositions — reducing demand for session musicians and certain compositional work. However, live performance, the irreplaceable human emotional connection of a singer or instrumentalist, and the cultural value placed on human artistry provide a meaningful barrier against full automation.

2653.rationale: Choreographers and dancers work primarily through physical embodiment, live performance, and kinesthetic creativity — core aspects that AI cannot replicate or automate. While AI tools can assist with notation, music selection, or video analysis, the fundamental value of the occupation lies in human movement, artistic expression, and live presence, creating a strong natural barrier to AI displacement.

2654.rationale: Directors and producers combine highly physical, on-set coordination work (rehearsals, shoots, live performances) with creative decision-making that requires human judgment, taste, and interpersonal leadership that AI cannot fully replicate. However, AI will increasingly assist with pre/post-production tasks like script analysis, scheduling, budgeting, editing, and even generative visual content, reducing the labor needed in those phases and moderately reshaping the overall role.

2655.rationale: Acting is fundamentally rooted in physical presence, emotional authenticity, and human embodiment that AI cannot yet replicate for most live and filmed performances, creating a natural barrier to full automation.
